The failures of others, whether into sin or ruin, warn us not to be secure or think too highly of ourselves. The prophet shows an example of someone like the king of Egypt in greatness, like the Assyrian compared to a tall cedar tree. Those who are better than others make themselves targets of envy; but the blessings of heaven are not subject to such flaws. The greatest security any creature can offer is like the shadow of a tree—a small and weak protection. But let us flee to God for safety, there we will be safe. His hand must be recognized in the rise of important people on earth, and we should not envy them. Although worldly people may seem to have lasting prosperity, it only appears so.

The king of Egypt resembled the king of Assyria in his greatness; here we see he resembles him in his pride. And he will resemble him in his fall. His own sin brings about his ruin. None of our comforts are ever lost, but what has been forfeited many times over. When great men fall, many fall with them, as many have fallen before. The fall of proud people is a warning to others, to keep them humble. See how low Pharaoh lies; and see what all his splendor and pride have become. It is best to be a humble tree of righteousness, producing fruit for the glory of God and for the benefit of people. The wicked person is often seen thriving like the cedar, and spreading like the green bay tree, but he soon disappears, and his place is no longer found. Let us then observe the blameless person, and behold the upright, because the end of that person is peace.
